text:
Circular economy (CE) research is a crucial lever for realising global sustainability and climate change adaptation. So far, however, the scientific community has largely focussed on economies in the Global North in high-income countries. This study therefore initiates a preliminary research agenda aimed at accelerating the implementation of the CE in the Global South, specifically on the African continent. Using a multi-method approach, based on a scoping literature review of 275 academic and practitioner-focused articles published in English and French, this study draws on a survey of 38 key industry stakeholders located in the countries that are part of the CE projects on the African continent.

South Africa is currently the main focus of CE research and practice, which calls for urgent investigations of other African countries to address the various socio-economic contexts existing in the continent. The literature review indicated that the predominant focus is on technical issues associated with waste management and recycling of materials (forward circulating materials), while overlooking the core principles of the Circular Economy (such as regenerative systems and design for prevention of waste). Challenges faced by researchers in their efforts to conduct ground-breaking research include lack of adequate funding to carry out the research, especially for organisations on the African continent, as well as lack of baseline information. The paper proposes a research framework based on three priorities; namely, private sector innovation, effective policy governance as well as socio-environmental assessment of e-waste and agrifood systems. The study forms part of a pioneering attempt in mapping the research focusing on the Circular Economy in Africa, hence enabling researchers and policy makers to progressively adopt sustainable alternatives, leading to the transition from linear economy models to a systems-based Circular Economy.
